tours.title: Architecture Overview
tours.description: A walkthrough of Pairpad's core components

steps.title: Daemon entry point
steps.description: The daemon CLI starts here. It supports 'serve', 'login', and 'version' commands. The 'serve' command is the main one — it connects to the server and exposes the local project.

steps.title: Server entry point
steps.description: The server is a stateless WebSocket relay. It accepts connections from daemons and browsers, and relays messages between them. Config comes from environment variables.

steps.title: Protocol messages
steps.description: All WebSocket messages are typed envelopes. This file defines every message type shared between daemon, server, and browser. Messages are JSON-encoded with a 'type' field for routing.

steps.title: Daemon connection loop
steps.description: The daemon connects to the server via WebSocket, sends the file tree, starts the filesystem watcher, and enters a loop handling server messages and local file events.

steps.title: Daemon WebSocket handler
steps.description: When a daemon connects, the server creates a session and relays messages between the daemon and any browsers that join. The server never stores code — it's a pure relay.

steps.title: Comment storage
steps.description: Comments are stored locally in .pairpad/comments.json. The daemon owns all comment state — the server only relays. Comments use anchor text for re-anchoring when code changes.

steps.title: Frontend entry point
steps.description: The browser IDE is built with CodeMirror 6 and bundled with esbuild. This file handles WebSocket connection, file tree, tabs, comments, guide mode, and tour mode.

tours.title: How to Add a Feature
tours.description: Step-by-step guide for adding a new message type and UI feature

steps.title: 1. Define the message type
steps.description: Add a new MessageType constant here. Follow the pattern: Browser → Server messages, Server → Daemon messages, etc. Then add the corresponding struct at the bottom of the file.

steps.title: 2. Handle it in the server
steps.description: The server's browser message loop is here. For most features, the server just relays to the daemon or broadcasts to browsers. Add a case for your new message type.
anchor_context: // Read messages from browser and relay to daemon

steps.title: 3. Handle it in the daemon
steps.description: If your feature involves the filesystem or local storage (like comments/tours), handle the message here. The daemon reads/writes local files and broadcasts results back through the server.
anchor_context: if err := d.handleServerMessage(ctx, conn, env); err != nil {

steps.title: 4. Handle it in the frontend
steps.description: The message handler switch statement is here. Parse the payload and call your rendering function. Keep the handler small — put the logic in a separate function below.
steps.anchor_text: // Also broadcast guide state on scroll (CodeMirror's scroller)
anchor_context: // Also broadcast guide state on scroll (CodeMirror's scroller)

steps.title: 5. Build and test
steps.description: 'make build' compiles everything: frontend (esbuild), server, and daemon. Run 'make server' and 'make daemon' in separate terminals, open the browser, and test your feature.

tours.title: Security Audit
tours.description: A guided walkthrough of Pairpad's security properties for auditors and security-conscious teams

steps.title: Minimal dependencies
steps.description: Pairpad has exactly 2 direct dependencies: coder/websocket (WebSocket protocol) and fsnotify (filesystem watcher). Plus one transitive dependency (golang.org/x/sys). The entire dependency tree is auditable in minutes. Compare this to Electron apps with hundreds of npm packages.

steps.title: Hard deny list — files never served
steps.description: These patterns are always denied regardless of configuration. .env files, SSH keys, GPG keys, PEM/key files, .git directory, and node_modules are never exposed over the WebSocket connection. This list cannot be overridden by .gitignore or .pairpadignore.
steps.anchor_text: // hardDenyPatterns are never served regardless of configuration.
anchor_context: // hardDenyPatterns are never served regardless of configuration.

steps.title: Path traversal prevention
steps.description: Any relative path containing '..' is rejected. Combined with filepath.Join() for all file operations (which normalizes paths), this prevents directory traversal attacks. A browser client cannot request files outside the project directory.
anchor_context: // shouldIgnore returns true if the given relative path matches any ignore pattern.

steps.title: File size limits
steps.description: Files larger than 5MB are silently skipped during tree scanning and file reads. This prevents memory exhaustion from serving large binary files or log files. The WebSocket read limit is set to 10MB on both sides.
anchor_context: // scanTree walks the project directory and returns a list of file entries,

steps.title: Wire protocol — JSON envelopes
steps.description: All messages are JSON-encoded with a 'type' field for routing and a 'payload' field containing the typed data. The protocol is stateless and inspectable — you can read every message on the wire with standard WebSocket debugging tools. No binary protocol, no obfuscation.
steps.anchor_text: // Encode wraps a typed payload into an Envelope for transmission.
anchor_context: // Encode wraps a typed payload into an Envelope for transmission.
func Encode(msgType MessageType, payload any) ([]byte, error) {

steps.title: Server is a stateless relay
steps.description: The server never stores code, comments, or project data. It holds ephemeral session state (who's connected, cursor positions) in memory only. When the server restarts, all sessions end. Code transits the server as WebSocket messages but is never written to disk or cached. The server cannot be subpoenaed for your source code because it doesn't have it.

steps.title: Comments stay local
steps.description: Comments and tours are stored in .pairpad/ inside your project directory. The daemon reads and writes them. The server only relays comment messages between the daemon and browsers during live sessions. When the session ends, the server retains nothing. Your annotations are your files on your machine.
anchor_context: // Number of context lines above and below the anchor

steps.title: Write serialization — no race conditions
steps.description: All WebSocket writes to the daemon are serialized through writeToDaemon() with mutex protection. Browser broadcasts copy the connection list under a read lock before iterating. This prevents concurrent write corruption and map iteration crashes.
steps.anchor_text: // broadcastToBrowsers sends a message to all connected browser clients.
anchor_context: // broadcastToBrowsers sends a message to all connected browser clients.

steps.title: Daemon connects outbound only
steps.description: The daemon initiates an outbound WebSocket connection to the server. No inbound ports are opened. No port forwarding, no firewall rules, no NAT traversal. The daemon works behind corporate firewalls because it's just an HTTPS connection going out.

steps.title: Frontend — no tracking, no analytics
steps.description: The browser IDE is a static bundle served from the Go binary. No third-party scripts, no CDN loads, no analytics, no cookies (only localStorage for your display name). The frontend connects to exactly one WebSocket endpoint — the server you told it to connect to. View source to verify.
